cards.title: Why Format Matters Underneath The Abstraction
cards.teaser: You usually think in tables and queries, not in bytes on disk. But every storage choice rests on a serialization format with specific cost, performance, and compatibility trade-offs.

runs.text: The format your data lives in dictates:

items.text: — JSON vs Parquet for the same data is often a 5-10× difference.
— column-oriented formats let queries skip irrelevant columns; row-oriented formats force full reads.
— some formats handle adding/removing fields gracefully; others require regeneration.
— every engine reads Parquet; not every engine reads ORC; very few read Avro natively.
— Avro's per-record framing makes it good for streams; Parquet's columnar structure makes it bad.
— column-oriented formats compress dramatically better than row-oriented.

runs.text: For most day-to-day data engineering, the choice is "Parquet for analytical storage, Avro for streaming records, JSON for human-debuggable transit." Knowing why those defaults exist — and when to deviate — is the substance of this appendix.
Data engineers who can't explain why Parquet beats CSV at scale tend to have warehouse bills they can't explain either.
Same as understanding file formats in software — you can write apps without it, but debugging is much harder when you don't.

cards.title: Row-Oriented Versus Column-Oriented Storage
cards.teaser: The most fundamental design choice in data formats. Determines what's fast, what's slow, and what's affordable to query.

runs.text: Row-oriented.
All values for one row stored contiguously.

explanation.code:
Row 1: [name=Ada,    age=37, city=Austin]
Row 2: [name=Linus,  age=54, city=Helsinki]
Row 3: [name=Grace,  age=42, city=NYC]

runs.text: Reading row 1 means reading one chunk: name + age + city together. Optimal for "give me everything about row 1" — the OLTP workload.
Column-oriented.
All values for one column stored contiguously.

explanation.code:
Names:   [Ada, Linus, Grace, ...]
Ages:    [37, 54, 42, ...]
Cities:  [Austin, Helsinki, NYC, ...]

runs.text: Reading "all names" means reading one contiguous chunk. Optimal for "compute average age" — analytical workloads scanning many rows but few columns.
Implications:

items.text: — column storage compresses dramatically better. The
column has many repeated values; the row stream interleaves cities with names and ages.
— analytical queries can read only the columns they need. Row formats force reading everything.
— row storage handles single-row inserts cleanly. Column storage prefers batch writes.

runs.text: The split:

items.text: — Avro, JSON, CSV (ish), protobuf. Used in OLTP, streaming, RPC.
— Parquet, ORC, Arrow (in-memory). Used in analytics, data lakes.

runs.text: If your warehouse is built on row-oriented storage, you're paying 10-50× more for analytical queries than you should. The fix is migrating to columnar, not tuning queries.
Same trade-off as struct-of-arrays vs array-of-structs in low-level programming. Choose the layout matching how you'll iterate.

cards.title: Parquet — The Columnar Default
cards.teaser: Parquet has become the de facto standard for analytical data. Knowing its structure explains why it's so much faster than CSV at scale.

runs.text: A Parquet file is structured as:

items.text: — subsets of rows (typically 100k-1M each), the unit of parallelism.
— within each row group, one chunk per column. Each is independently compressed and encoded.
— within column chunks, smaller units (typically 1MB) that are individually encoded.
— metadata: schema, row group offsets, statistics for each column chunk (min, max, null count).

runs.text: Why this matters:

items.text: reads the footer first, skips row groups whose date column max is below the threshold. Only relevant row groups are read from disk.
reads only the name column chunks. The rest stays on disk.
Different row groups can be read by different threads or workers.
Strings dictionary-encoded; integers run-length-encoded; floats bit-packed. Each column gets the encoding that compresses it best.

runs.text: Statistics in the footer make the difference. A 10TB Parquet table with good column statistics often serves analytical queries reading just GB of actual data.
When designing Parquet layouts, sort by the columns most often filtered. Sorting concentrates value ranges in fewer row groups, making predicate pushdown maximally effective.
Same idea as a book index — read the index first, jump to the relevant pages, ignore everything else.

cards.title: ORC, Avro, And Other Formats
cards.teaser: Beyond Parquet, several other formats serve specific niches. Knowing which fits which use case prevents wasted effort.

runs.text: ORC (Optimized Row Columnar).
Columnar like Parquet; originated in the Hortonworks/Hive ecosystem. Similar performance characteristics. Less common in cloud-native workflows; still seen in Hadoop-heritage stacks.
Avro.

Row-oriented binary format with embedded schema. Each record carries enough metadata to deserialize. Designed for streaming and RPC. The default for Kafka payloads in many setups, often with a schema registry to avoid embedding the full schema in every message.
Wins: schema evolution is well-defined; RPC-friendly; per-record processing. Losses: not analytical-query-friendly; no column pruning.
Protobuf (Protocol Buffers).
Google's RPC serialization format. Highly compact, schema-defined, fast serialize/deserialize. Used heavily in microservices and Google-flavored systems.
Wins: smallest wire format; strongly typed; great cross-language support. Losses: schema lives outside the data (in
.proto
files); not human-readable; not analytical-friendly.
Apache Arrow.

In-memory columnar format. Not for storage; for analytical processing. Many engines (DuckDB, Pandas 2.x, Polars, Spark via Arrow) use it as their internal representation. Eliminates serialization cost between Arrow-aware tools.
JSON / JSONL.
Text-based; schemaless; ubiquitous. Fine for APIs, configs, small datasets, debugging. Terrible for analytical storage at scale.
CSV.
Plain text rows. Fine for human-readable export. Awful for almost everything else — no types, no schema, ambiguous parsing rules, terrible compression.
Modern stacks use a small mix: Parquet at rest, Avro in streams, JSON for APIs, Arrow internally. CSV only for humans.
Same toolbox as encodings in software — different tools for different jobs; misuse is a common source of pain.

cards.title: Compression Algorithms — The Speed-Versus-Ratio Trade
cards.teaser: All compression sits on a curve from "fast and modest ratio" to "slow but tight." Picking right depends on whether your bottleneck is CPU, disk, or network.

runs.text: The major algorithms in data systems:

items.text: Google's; very fast compress/decompress; modest ratio (~1.5-2.5×). Default for many engines because decompression doesn't slow queries.
Even faster than Snappy; similar ratio. Good when CPU is the bottleneck.
Older; slow compress, decent ratio (3-4×). Common for archive data; less common for hot tables.
Modern; tunable level (1-22). At default levels, faster than gzip with better ratio. Often the best general-purpose choice today.
Designed for web; high ratio, slow compress. Common for static content delivery.
Highest ratios; very slow compress; moderate decompress. Used for cold archive.

runs.text: Where each fits in data systems:

items.text: — Snappy or Zstd at low level. Decompression speed matters more than tightest ratio.
— Zstd at high level or Gzip. Ratio matters; access is rare.
— Gzip or Zstd. Decent ratio, broadly compatible.

runs.text: A common pattern: Zstd has been quietly displacing Gzip and even Snappy in many workloads — better ratio than Snappy, much faster than Gzip at default settings, tunable when needed.
Most analytical workloads are I/O-bound, not CPU-bound. Spending CPU on better compression to save I/O is usually a net win — which is why Zstd and Snappy beat "no compression" for almost everything.
Same trade-off as image compression — JPEG vs PNG vs WebP. Different sweet spots for different content and access patterns.

cards.title: Column Encodings Within Parquet
cards.teaser: Parquet doesn't just compress columns — it encodes them per-column based on what's most efficient for that data shape.

runs.text: Inside a Parquet column chunk, several encodings combine before final compression:

items.text: Just the raw values, one after another. Used for high-cardinality columns where nothing smarter helps.
Build a dictionary of unique values; replace each occurrence with a small integer index. Optimal for low-cardinality columns (country codes, status enums, repeated strings).
When a column has runs of identical values, encode as
When integer values fit in fewer bits than a full int, pack them. A column of values 0-7 only needs 3 bits each.
For sorted or near-sorted numeric columns, store the difference from the previous value.

runs.text: Then on top of all that, the encoded column chunk is compressed (Snappy, Zstd, etc.).
The result: a column of 1B repeated country codes might land at a few KB on disk. The Parquet writer chooses encoding per column automatically; you usually don't tune it manually.

Compression ratio of 10-100× on well-shaped analytical data is normal. If your Parquet files are barely smaller than the source CSV, your data has high cardinality everywhere or your writer is misconfigured.
Same magic as compressing log files — repeated
INFO
prefixes, timestamps that monotonically increase, mostly-the-same source patterns. The compression algorithm finds the redundancy.

cards.title: Schema Evolution In Practice
cards.teaser: Different formats handle schema changes very differently. Picking a format that supports the changes you'll need saves expensive migrations later.

runs.text: What schema evolution scenarios you'll face:

items.text: Should be safe; readers ignore unknown columns or get null for missing ones.
Old data still has the column; new data doesn't.
Treat as drop + add; old data has the old name.
Often unsafe; readers expect specific types.

runs.text: How major formats handle these:
Parquet:

items.text: Schema lives in the file footer; readers detect and adapt.
Adding columns is safe; readers get nulls for old files missing the column.
Renaming is harder — newer Iceberg/Delta layers (column IDs) add support; raw Parquet doesn't track renames.
Type changes generally require rewriting affected files.

runs.text: Avro:

items.text: Schema-on-write embedded in each file (or referenced from a schema registry).
Reader and writer schemas can differ; resolution rules handle adds, drops, renames (with aliases).
Type changes have well-defined compatibility rules.
Strongest schema-evolution story of the major formats.

runs.text: Iceberg / Delta:

items.text: Maintain table metadata that handles evolution at a table level above Parquet.
Column IDs decouple field identity from name — renames work cleanly.
Hidden partitioning lets you change partition strategy without rewriting data.

runs.text: JSON / JSONL:

items.text: No enforced schema; evolution is "just write the new shape."
Readers must handle missing or unexpected fields.

runs.text: If you expect schema evolution (you will), prefer Iceberg or Delta over raw Parquet. The metadata layer is what makes evolution survivable.
Same problem as API versioning — backward compatibility is achievable with discipline, painful without.

cards.title: Format Choices For Streaming Data
cards.teaser: Streaming workflows have different format requirements from analytical storage. Picking the right one prevents the most common streaming pain.

runs.text: Streaming systems handle data record-by-record (or in small micro-batches). What this needs from a format:

items.text: Reader can decode individual records without reading the whole file.
Network bandwidth and broker storage matter.
Producers and consumers must agree on shape; ideally enforced.
Producers and consumers deploy independently; messages from old producers must remain readable.

runs.text: Formats that fit:

items.text: Default for Kafka in many setups. Per-record framing; embedded schema or schema-registry reference; mature compatibility model.
Compact, fast, well-supported. Schema lives in
files; requires distribution to all producers/consumers.
Easy for debugging, no compatibility tooling, larger wire size. Common for early-stage Kafka usage; teams usually migrate away as scale grows.

runs.text: Schema registry adds:

items.text: for schemas — produce against a registered schema; consumers fetch the schema by ID embedded in each message.
— incompatible schema changes are rejected at registration time.
— messages carry a 5-byte schema ID instead of the full schema.

runs.text: The trap to avoid: starting with JSON-on-Kafka for convenience and ending up with a coordination problem when the team grows. Schema registry + Avro from the start is more discipline; less pain later.
If your Kafka setup is more than 10 producers / 10 consumers, you need a schema registry. Without one, every breaking change is a coordinated production incident.
Same lesson as REST APIs — formal schema contracts (OpenAPI, JSON Schema) save you from coordination problems that grow with team size.
